title: W11: VAEs, Generative Vision Models, and Late Topics
blurb: High priority inside the vision block. VAE likelihood, ELBO, and reparameterization appear in 2024, 2025, and 2026.

knowledge_points.title: VAE Likelihood and Intractability
knowledge_points.recap: VAEs introduce a latent random variable z with a prior $p(z)$ and decoder likelihood $p_\theta(x\mid z)$. Direct marginal likelihood requires an integral over latent space.

questions.q: 4. Let $x$ denote an input image and $\theta$ the model parameters. Write down the data likelihood $p_\theta(x)$ for a variational autoencoder. Explain what each term represents and why this objective cannot be optimized directly. (2 points)
questions.answer:
The data likelihood is **(0.5 points)**

$$p_\theta(x)=\int p_\theta(z)p_\theta(x\mid z)\,dz.$$

Here **$p_\theta(z)$ is the prior over the latent vector $z$**, modeled as a multivariate Gaussian $N(0,I)$ **(0.5 points)**, and **$p_\theta(x\mid z)$ is the conditional likelihood of the data given latent vector $z$**, represented by a decoder neural network **(0.5 points)**. The integral is **intractable**, because it requires integrating over the entire high-dimensional latent space **(0.5 points)**.
questions.extend: Memory: VAE likelihood marginalizes latent $z$; direct optimization is hard because the integral over latent space is intractable.

questions.q: 3. Why are standard autoencoders not used for image generation? Describe the image-generating process in variational autoencoders. (2 points)
questions.answer:
**Standard autoencoders use deterministic mappings** from input space $x$ to latent space $z$ and back. Once trained, the encoder-decoder pair can reconstruct inputs similar to the training data but cannot generate new diverse samples. **It is not possible to sample from the latent space since it is not known.**

Using variational autoencoders, **we sample from the latent space with the prior distribution $p_\theta(z)$**. The generated image is then a sample taken from **$p_\theta(x\mid z)$**.
questions.extend: Memory: deterministic AE has no known sampleable latent prior; VAE gives a prior and decoder likelihood for generation.

questions.q: Why is the marginal likelihood $p_\theta(x)=\int p_\theta(x|z)p(z)\,dz$ usually intractable in a VAE?
questions.answer: The decoder is a neural network, so the integral over all latent variables usually has no closed-form solution. The latent space may be continuous and high-dimensional, making exact integration impractical. VAEs therefore optimize a tractable lower bound, the ELBO, instead of the exact marginal likelihood.
questions.extend: Memory: VAE likelihood is hard because it integrates the neural decoder over latent space.

questions.q: How does a VAE differ from a deterministic autoencoder at generation time?
questions.answer: A deterministic autoencoder usually needs an encoded input to produce a reconstruction. A VAE learns a latent prior, commonly $p(z)=\mathcal{N}(0,I)$, so generation can sample $z$ from the prior and decode it into a new data sample. This makes the latent space explicitly generative.
questions.extend: Memory: VAE generation samples latent variables from a prior, then decodes.

knowledge_points.title: ELBO and Reparameterization Trick
knowledge_points.recap: Training VAEs uses the ELBO: a reconstruction te$rm$ minus a KL te$rm$. Reparameterization makes sampling differentiable with respect to encoder parameters.

questions.q:
Assume you are given a dataset $D=\{x^{(i)}\}_{i=1}^N$ and train a variational autoencoder using the ELBO

$$L(\theta,\phi;x^{(i)})=\mathbb{E}_{z\sim q_\phi(z\mid x^{(i)})}[\log p_\theta(x^{(i)}\mid z)]-D_{KL}(q_\phi(z\mid x^{(i)})\,\|\,p(z)).$$

(a) Explain why the expectation in the ELBO is generally intractable, and how it is approximated during training. (2 points)
questions.answer:
The expectation

$$\mathbb{E}_{z\sim q_\phi(z\mid x^{(i)})}[\log p_\theta(x^{(i)}\mid z)]$$

is intractable because it involves **marginalizing over all possible latent vectors $z$**, which would require evaluating the decoder over the entire latent space **(1 point)**. During training, the expectation is approximated using **Monte Carlo sampling** by drawing samples of $z$ from $q_\phi(z\mid x^{(i)})$ **(1 point)**.
questions.extend: Memory: expectation over latent space is approximated by sampling from the encoder distribution.

questions.q: (b) Explain how the reparameterization trick allows backpropagation through the ELBO with respect to the encoder parameters $\phi$. (2 points)
questions.answer:
The encoder $q_\phi(z\mid x^{(i)})$ outputs the parameters of a Gaussian distribution: **mean $\mu_\phi(x^{(i)})$ and standard deviation $\sigma_\phi(x^{(i)})$ (1 point)**. Sampling $z\sim N(\mu_\phi(x^{(i)}),\sigma_\phi(x^{(i)})^2I)$ directly is non-differentiable with respect to $\phi$.

The reparameterization trick samples **$\epsilon\sim N(0,I)$** and computes

$$z=\mu_\phi(x^{(i)})+\sigma_\phi(x^{(i)})\cdot\epsilon,$$

which allows differentiating the ELBO with respect to encoder parameters during the forward pass **(1 point)**.
questions.extend: Memory: move randomness into $\epsilon$; keep $\mu_\phi$ and $\sigma_\phi$ in a differentiable computation graph.

questions.q: 2. A transposed convolution, also called upconvolution, is used to increase the spatial dimensions of a feature map. Given an input feature map of size $2\times2$, a filter of size $3\times3$, stride 2, and padding 1, what is the spatial size of the output feature map? (1 point)
questions.extend: Memory: use the convention from the exam statement/solution; expected answer is simply $4\times4$.

questions.q: What are the reconstruction and KL terms in the VAE ELBO encouraging?
questions.answer: The reconstruction term encourages decoded samples from the approximate posterior to explain the input well. The KL term encourages the approximate posterior $q_\phi(z|x)$ to stay close to the prior $p(z)$. Together, they balance data reconstruction against a smooth, sampleable latent space.
questions.extend: Memory: reconstruction means fit input; KL means regularize latent distribution toward prior.

questions.q: Why does the reparameterization trick write $z=\mu_\phi(x)+\sigma_\phi(x)\odot\epsilon$ with $\epsilon\sim\mathcal{N}(0,I)$?
questions.answer: This separates the random sampling from the learnable parameters. The noise $\epsilon$ is sampled independently, while $\mu_\phi(x)$ and $\sigma_\phi(x)$ remain differentiable functions of the encoder parameters. Gradients can therefore backpropagate through $z$ into the encoder.
questions.extend: Memory: move randomness into $\epsilon$ so gradients flow through $\mu$ and $\sigma$.
